title: In-Group / Out-Group Formation
description: Tajfel's minimal group paradigm made visible — how arbitrary categories create loyalty, bias, and discrimination.

boundary_strength.label: Boundary Strength
boundary_strength.description: How strongly group membership drives behaviour

narration.text: Two groups — blue and orange. The division is completely arbitrary: Tajfel (1971) created groups by coin flip and still found immediate favouritism toward one's own group.
Within-group cooperation increases. Blue individuals help blue neighbours. Orange helps orange. Resources flow within groups, not between them.
Inter-group contact triggers avoidance. The groups are clustering spatially — a physical boundary forming from an invisible psychological one.
Each group now has different information, different resources, different norms. The initial arbitrary division has become a real social difference.
This is social identity theory. People derive self-esteem from their group's status — so they're motivated to see their group as better. Bias emerges from ordinary psychology, not hatred.
Contact theory (Allport, 1954): in-group/out-group bias weakens when groups cooperate toward shared goals under equal status. Division is learned. It can be unlearned.
